data.title: Chevron News Highlights Rising Geopolitical Risks And Portfolio Repositioning
data.description:
• Chevron declared force majeure at the Leviathan gas field in Israel, affecting Mediterranean gas supplies and regional energy security.
  • The company agreed to pay more than $1 million to settle a U.S. Department of Justice case related to renewable fuel credit compliance.
  • Chevron reached a deal to sell its operated interests in certain Angolan oil blocks to Energean.
  • The company is progressing petroleum agreements in Venezuela while geopolitical risks remain elevated.
  • These developments come after a share purchase by major investor Berkshire Hathaway.

For investors watching NYSE:CVX, these moves arrive while the stock trades at $196.97 and has returned 3.7% over the past week and 8.1% over the past month. Over longer periods, returns of 26.3% year to date and 34.0% over 1 year present Chevron as a company whose share price has been responsive to recent company and sector news.

What stands out now is how operational disruptions, legal settlements, and portfolio reshaping across Israel, the U.S., Angola, and Venezuela intersect for one of the largest listed energy companies. As you weigh Chevron’s mix of risks and opportunities, these developments highlight where its capital, regulatory attention, and geopolitical exposure are currently concentrated.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Chevron by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Chevron.

📰 Beyond the headline: 2 risks and 2 things going right for Chevron that every investor should see.

The force majeure at Leviathan, the environmental compliance settlement, and the portfolio moves in Angola and Venezuela all point in the same direction for Chevron, which is to concentrate on assets and regions it wants to own for the long haul while accepting higher operational and regulatory complexity. Leviathan’s suspension shows how exposed Eastern Mediterranean gas is to security decisions, and it may temporarily affect gas-linked cash flows and contract relationships with buyers in Israel, Egypt, and Jordan. By contrast, selling offshore Angola interests to Energean and progressing Venezuelan agreements suggests Chevron is recycling capital from a mature African position into heavier crude and longer-life projects in the Orinoco Belt. The U.S. renewable fuel credit case, while small in dollar terms relative to Chevron’s size, is a reminder that compliance systems and environmental obligations are an ongoing cost of doing business, especially compared with peers like ExxonMobil and Shell. For you as an investor, the combined picture is a company leaning into complex, partnership-heavy projects while carrying both geopolitical and regulatory risk that needs to be weighed against its integrated scale.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Chevron Narrative

  • The push to deepen Venezuelan exposure, sell certain Angolan blocks, and keep large gas projects like Leviathan central to the portfolio ties in with the narrative that Chevron is leaning on long-lived, low-cost upstream positions and acquisitions to support future cash generation and dividend resilience.
  • Force majeure at Leviathan and the U.S. renewable fuel credit settlement highlight execution, regulatory, and geopolitical risks that sit alongside the narrative’s focus on growth from the Permian, Guyana, and Venezuela, and may challenge assumptions about smooth production and regulatory stability.
  • The specific compliance issues under the Renewable Fuel Standard and the near term cash flow and contract impact of Leviathan’s shutdown are not fully reflected in the broader narrative, even though both can influence how investors think about future margins and risk-adjusted returns across Chevron’s portfolio.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Chevron to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Greater exposure to high risk jurisdictions such as Venezuela, along with conflict affected areas like Israel and wider Middle East routes, could lead to more frequent production interruptions, contract renegotiations, or changes to fiscal terms.
  • ⚠️ The environmental compliance settlement under the U.S. Renewable Fuel Standard underscores ongoing regulatory and ESG scrutiny that can add to costs and may feed into concerns analysts already flag about dividend coverage and insider selling.
  • 🎁 Recycling capital from offshore Angola into higher impact projects in Venezuela and elsewhere can sharpen Chevron’s focus on assets it views as core to long term cash generation compared with global peers such as ExxonMobil and BP.
  • 🎁 Maintaining a broad, integrated portfolio across oil, gas, and LNG, even with local disruptions at Leviathan, gives Chevron multiple cash flow sources, which can help it manage through regional shocks while it pursues longer-dated projects.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it makes sense to watch how long Leviathan production remains constrained and whether there are any changes to sales contracts or expansion timelines for that field. You may also want to track progress on Venezuelan agreements, including any details on royalties, taxes, and export conditions, and how quickly Chevron closes the Angolan divestment and redeploys that capital. Finally, keep an eye on further regulatory disclosures around renewable fuels and broader ESG matters, especially if you are already following existing analyst concerns about dividend coverage and insider transactions.

data.title: Kamakura Shinsho (TSE:6184) Margin Slippage Tests Bullish Earnings Growth Narrative
data.description:
Kamakura Shinsho (TSE:6184) has wrapped up FY 2026 with fourth quarter revenue of ¥2,247.1 million and basic EPS of ¥4.05, capping a twelve month run where earnings grew 11.4% and the net margin sat at 9.2%. The company has seen revenue move from ¥7,061 million to ¥8,335 million on a trailing twelve month basis, while basic EPS has shifted from ¥18.54 to ¥20.49, with five year earnings growth averaging 21.6% a year. For investors, that combination of expanding earnings over several years and a slightly softer margin profile sets up a results season story that is as much about profitability quality as it is about the headline growth rates.

See our full analysis for Kamakura Shinsho.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this mix of growth and margin pressure lines up with the prevailing market narratives around Kamakura Shinsho and where those stories might need an update.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM revenue reaches ¥8,335 million

  • Over the last twelve months, Kamakura Shinsho generated ¥8,335 million in revenue and ¥765 million in net income, giving that 9.2% net margin that investors are now comparing with the 9.7% margin a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is that earnings grew 11.4% over the year and five year earnings growth averaged 21.6% a year. However, the small step down in margin to 9.2% means supporters have to balance solid profit expansion against slightly thinner profitability.
    • Supporters often point to consistent earnings growth as a sign of quality, and the 11.4% increase over the last twelve months lines up with that argument.
    • At the same time, the move from a 9.7% to 9.2% net margin shows that growth has not been cost free, which keeps attention on how sustainable that profit profile is over time.
To see how retail and institutional investors are interpreting this mix of growth and margins across different viewpoints, take a look at 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Kamakura Shinsho. for a fuller picture.

P/E of 25.1x versus industry 16.9x

  • The shares trade on a 25.1x trailing P/E at a price of ¥490, compared with the broader JP Interactive Media and Services industry at 16.9x and a peer group average of 56.2x, so the company sits between the industry and its closer peers on this simple valuation yardstick.
  • Critics highlight that paying 25.1x earnings when DCF fair value is cited at ¥284.34 means the current price is above that modelled value. This tension between the higher multiple and the DCF fair value gives cautious investors a clear reference point when they think about how much of the 11.4% earnings growth is already reflected in the share price.
    • The gap between the ¥490 price and the ¥284.34 DCF fair value is one reason some investors describe the shares as expensive relative to a cash flow model, even though the multiple is still below the 56.2x peer average.
    • Because the trailing P/E also sits above the 16.9x industry average, bears can argue that buyers are paying more than the sector norm while also being reminded that one valuation model points to a lower fair value.

4.08% dividend with weak cash coverage

  • The stock offers a 4.08% dividend yield, but the dividend is described as weakly covered by free cash flow, so the payout level is not fully backed by the cash the business produced over the same period.
  • Investors who focus on income point to the 4.08% yield as attractive, yet the comment about weak free cash flow coverage sits awkwardly next to earnings growth of 11.4% and five year earnings growth of 21.6% a year, because it suggests that accounting profits have been stronger than the cash that ultimately supports dividends.
    • A key risk flagged is that if free cash flow stays tight, maintaining that 4.08% yield could become harder, even though net income for the year reached ¥765 million.
    • Income focused holders therefore need to weigh the history of earnings expansion against the explicit warning that cash coverage of the current dividend has been weak over the last twelve months.

data.title: Is It Time To Reconsider Mattel (MAT) After The Recent Share Price Slide
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Mattel's current share price reflects its true worth, this article will walk through what the numbers are saying about the stock's value.
  • Mattel recently closed at US$16.22, with returns of 1.2% over 7 days, a 23.0% decline over 30 days, and year to date and 1 year returns of 19.1% and 17.5% declines respectively. These moves may have shifted how the market views both its potential and its risks.
  • Recent coverage around Mattel has focused on its position in the branded toys and entertainment space and how investor sentiment reacts when expectations around its franchises change. These moves in the share price have kept attention on how much of Mattel's long term potential is already reflected in the current valuation.
  • On our checklist of six valuation tests, Mattel scores a 5 out of 6, which suggests the stock screens as undervalued on most metrics. We will walk through those methods next before finishing with a more complete way to think about valuation overall.

Find out why Mattel's -17.5% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Mattel Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company may be worth by projecting the cash it could generate in the future and then discounting those cash flows back to today using a required return. It is essentially asking what all those future dollars are worth in current terms.

For Mattel, the model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The company’s last twelve months free cash flow is about $413.0 million. Analysts provide explicit free cash flow estimates out to 2028, where projected free cash flow is $435.65 million, and the remaining years out to 2035 are extrapolated based on those earlier expectations.

When all these projected cash flows are discounted back and combined, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $28.78 per share. Against the recent share price of $16.22, this implies an intrinsic discount of roughly 43.6%, which means the stock screens as undervalued on this DCF view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Mattel is undervalued by 43.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 47 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Mattel.

Approach 2: Mattel Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like Mattel, the P/E ratio is a useful way to look at value because it directly links what you pay for each share to the company’s current earnings. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future earnings growth and how risky they think those earnings are.

Mattel’s current P/E is 12.33x. That sits below both the Leisure industry average of 21.05x and the peer group average of 43.01x, which suggests the market is pricing Mattel’s earnings more cautiously than many of its peers.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Mattel is 16.25x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/E could be given factors such as the company’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and risk indicators. Because it incorporates these company specific inputs, the Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple comparison to industry or peer averages, which can mix very different types of businesses together.

Comparing Mattel’s actual P/E of 12.33x to the Fair Ratio of 16.25x, the shares appear undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 18 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Mattel Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. Narratives let you attach a clear story about Mattel’s future to the numbers you think are fair for its revenue, earnings and margins, link that story to a Fair Value, and then compare it with the current share price on Simply Wall St’s Community page. This means you can see, for example, how one investor might build a cautious Mattel Narrative around a Fair Value of US$14 while another builds an optimistic one around US$29. Both Narratives then automatically refresh as new news or earnings arrive to help each investor decide if the price looks attractive or extended against their own view.

For Mattel however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Mattel Narratives:

🐂 Mattel Bull Case

Fair Value: US$29

Implied discount vs recent price: about 44%

Revenue growth assumption: 3.67%

  • Focuses on global expansion, digital play, and entertainment licensing as key drivers of future revenue mix and brand reach.
  • Assumes steady earnings progression with a higher future P/E multiple than today, yet still below current US Leisure peers.
  • Highlights execution risks around digital shifts, demographics, sustainability costs, and competition, and urges you to stress test analyst assumptions against your own view.

🐻 Mattel Bear Case

Fair Value: US$14

Implied premium vs recent price: about 15%

Revenue growth assumption: 2.23%

  • Centers on headwinds from more time spent on digital entertainment, lower birth rates, and higher regulatory costs weighing on long run toy demand and margins.
  • Builds in more cautious revenue growth and profitability, with a lower future P/E multiple than industry levels and a Fair Value of US$14.
  • Acknowledges strengths such as supply chain diversification, well known brands, cost savings programs, and a solid cash position, but views execution risk as high enough to limit upside.

data.title: NFI Group (TSX:NFI) Swings To US$166 Million Q4 Profit Testing Bullish Recovery Narrative
data.description:
NFI Group (TSX:NFI) closed out FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$1.0b and basic EPS of US$1.38, while on a trailing twelve month basis the company generated US$3.6b of revenue and basic EPS of US$1.19. The business has seen revenue move from US$837.0m in Q4 2024 to US$1.0b in Q4 2025, with quarterly basic EPS shifting from US$0.16 to US$1.38 over the same period. Investors will be looking closely at how these figures feed through to margin resilience and the path toward more stable profitability.

See our full analysis for NFI Group.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to line these results up against the key stories investors have been following to see which narratives hold up and which ones need a rethink.

See what the community is saying about NFI Group

Advertisement

Sharp swing from losses to profit in 2025

  • Across FY 2025, NFI moved from quarterly net losses of US$160.8 million in Q2 and US$140.9 million in Q3 to net income of US$166.0 million in Q4, with basic EPS shifting from a loss of US$1.35 in Q2 and US$1.18 in Q3 to earnings of US$1.38 in Q4.
  • What stands out for the bullish narrative is that this Q4 profit arrives while trailing 12 month figures still show a net loss of US$142.1 million and basic EPS of a loss of US$1.19, so:
    • Bulls who focus on the record US$13.2b backlog and higher average selling prices see Q4 as early support for their view that operational issues and supply chain pressures can ease over time, helping margins recover off the loss making LTM base.
    • At the same time, the fact that the LTM result remains in loss territory gives you a clear reminder that one strong quarter does not on its own settle the bullish case about more stable profitability.
If you are trying to work out whether this jump in profitability fits the optimistic story around backlog, margins and zero emission demand, it is worth seeing how bullish investors connect these numbers to their longer term expectations. 🐂 NFI Group Bull Case

Revenue at US$3.6b while margins still in the red

  • On a trailing 12 month basis to Q4 2025, NFI reported revenue of US$3.6b alongside a net loss of US$142.1 million, meaning the business is generating sizeable sales while overall profitability for the year is still negative.
  • Bears argue that this combination of high revenue and ongoing losses backs their concern that execution risks and cost pressures could keep margins under strain, and the LTM profile lines up with that caution because:
    • Losses over the past five years are reported to have grown at about 9.1% per year, which is consistent with the idea that the company has struggled to turn revenue into sustainable profits even as its top line expanded.
    • Ongoing items such as the US$229.9 million battery recall provision and supplier related issues in seats and batteries, as described in the bearish narrative, are the kind of factors that can keep cash flow and margins under pressure even when the order book is strong.
If you are focusing on these risks and want to see how cautious investors frame them in more detail, the full bearish case sets out how issues like recalls, tariffs and supplier concentration could affect future earnings. 🐻 NFI Group Bear Case

Low P/S multiple against DCF fair value and targets

  • With the share price at CA$16.94, NFI is trading on a P/S of 0.4x compared with 1.3x for peers and 2.0x for the wider North American Machinery group, while the DCF fair value of CA$44.21 and an analyst price target of CA$23.10 both sit above the current price.
  • Analysts’ and bulls’ narratives that talk about a path to profitability within three years and forecast earnings growth of about 80.9% a year are effectively being tested against this gap because:
    • Supporters point to the combination of a low 0.4x P/S multiple and forecasts for revenue growth of about 10.7% a year and improving margins as reasons why the current price could be out of step with those valuation markers.
    • On the other hand, the fact that the LTM net loss is still US$142.1 million means the market may be giving weight to the five year history of losses, which helps explain why the share price sits below both the DCF fair value of CA$44.21 and the CA$23.10 analyst target.

Next Steps

To see how these results tie into long-term growth, risks, and valuation, check out the full range of community narratives for NFI Group on Simply Wall St. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio so you'll be alerted when the story evolves.

After reading both the bull and bear angles, do you feel the balance of risks and rewards is clear enough yet, or is it still open to interpretation? It helps to move quickly, review the underlying numbers yourself and test how they line up with the areas investors are most optimistic about, especially the 4 key rewards.

See What Else Is Out There

data.title: Eli Lilly’s China Bet On Orforglipron Reshapes Obesity Drug Narrative
data.description:
• Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) announced a US$3b, decade-long plan to localize production and expand supply chain capacity for its oral obesity drug candidate, orforglipron, in China.
  • The company is partnering with Pharmaron for manufacturing and has filed orforglipron with Chinese regulators as part of an integrated, China-focused value chain.

Eli Lilly, known for treatments in diabetes, obesity care and other chronic conditions, is pushing deeper into obesity medicine with oral GLP-1 candidates such as orforglipron. The new China-focused investment comes amid strong global interest in weight management drugs and broader efforts by pharmaceutical companies to secure reliable production for high-demand therapies.

For you as an investor, this move highlights how Eli Lilly is tying manufacturing, partnerships and regulatory filings together in a single market entry plan. The scale and length of the commitment in China may influence how you think about the company’s international footprint, exposure to Asian healthcare markets and potential execution risks around a large, multi-year build out.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Eli Lilly by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Eli Lilly.

📰 Beyond the headline: 2 risks and 4 things going right for Eli Lilly that every investor should see.

This US$3b, decade-long commitment in China signals that Eli Lilly is treating orforglipron as a core global product rather than a side bet. By localizing oral solid-dose manufacturing and locking in capacity through Pharmaron, Lilly is trying to reduce supply risk and shorten distribution routes for China and potentially other Asian markets. For you, the key point is that this is not just about one drug; it is a build-out of a China-focused value chain that could later support other small-molecule medicines. At the same time, it adds execution questions, including regulatory timelines, local partner performance, and policy shifts that could affect foreign pharma in China. The move sits alongside Lilly’s U.S. access work for GLP-1 drugs, such as Employer Connect, so you are seeing both demand-side and supply-side investments around obesity care. How well Lilly balances capital going into China, Korea Gateway Labs and Japanese capacity with legal and pricing pressures at home will influence how durable this obesity-focused business model looks over time.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Eli Lilly Narrative

  • The China build-out supports the narrative theme that obesity and diabetes therapies anchor Lilly’s future. Global manufacturing expansion and supply chain hubs are described as key enablers of cardiometabolic growth.
  • Heavy capital spending tied to a small group of GLP-1 products also echoes a concern in the narrative around concentration risk, as more of Lilly’s cash and attention go toward a single therapy class.
  • The plan to use China as an Asian base for orforglipron and other pipeline drugs adds geographic and operational detail that the narrative only touches on at a high level when it talks about global expansion.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Eli Lilly to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Concentrating billions of dollars of new capacity in China adds country-specific policy, regulatory and geopolitical risk that could affect utilization or profitability over a long time frame.
  • ⚠️ Analysts already flag a high level of non-cash earnings, and layering large, multi-year projects on top of that can make it harder for you to judge the quality and timing of reported profits and cash flows.
  • 🎁 A localized supply chain for orforglipron could help Eli Lilly compete more effectively with peers such as Novo Nordisk and Pfizer if it supports reliable volumes and more flexible pricing in Asian markets.
  • 🎁 Reusing the new Chinese infrastructure for other oral therapies in Lilly’s pipeline may spread fixed costs over more products, which could support long-term operational efficiency if execution goes to plan.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it makes sense to watch three things closely: the progress of orforglipron’s regulatory review in China, any updates on construction milestones and utilization plans for the Suzhou and Pharmaron-linked facilities, and how management talks about capital allocation across China, Korea Gateway Labs, Japanese expansion and U.S. plants. You might also track whether competitors such as Novo Nordisk announce similar China-centric capacity moves, and how payors and regulators treat oral GLP-1 pricing versus injectables over time. Those signals can help you assess whether this US$3b commitment is building a flexible regional hub or focusing Lilly on a narrower GLP-1 opportunity.

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a bank can earn above the return that equity investors require, then links that to growth in book value over time. Instead of focusing on short term earnings swings, it uses steady state assumptions for profitability and capital.

For Banc of California, the model starts with a Book Value of $19.57 per share and a Stable EPS of $1.94 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 8 analysts. data.title: Volkswagen (XTRA:VOW3) Valuation Check As Deep Job Cuts And Profit Slump Reshape The Investment Case
data.description:
Volkswagen (XTRA:VOW3) is back in focus after announcing plans to cut 50,000 jobs in Germany by 2030, following a sharp profit drop in 2025 and a lower proposed dividend for shareholders.

See our latest analysis for Volkswagen.

The job cuts, weaker 2025 profit and lower proposed dividend help explain why the 30 day share price return is 11.64% lower and the year to date share price return is 13.22% lower, while the 5 year total shareholder return of 35.67% lower suggests longer term momentum has been fading.

If this kind of restructuring story has you looking across the auto and industrial space, it could be a good moment to widen your search with our screener of 98 top founder-led companies.

With earnings under pressure, a lower dividend on the table and the share price well off recent levels, investors now face a simple question: is Volkswagen trading at a discount, or has the market already priced in its future growth potential?

Advertisement

Most Popular Narrative: 35.1% Overvalued

According to the most followed narrative, Volkswagen's fair value sits at €68.40 versus the last close at €92.38, putting that narrative firmly on the cautious side.

It is one of the three biggest carmakers in the world, with a dominant market share of some 21 per cent in its home market, which actually grew in the recent past. Yet both from a strategic and a global perspective, the Wolfsburgers are under marked pressure. The corporation is among those traditional German industrial behemoths that got used to a comfortable market position, complete with above-average employee compensation and lavish management structures. In its sometimes arrogant self-assuredness, VW first perpetrated one of the biggest corporate scandals in German economic history when it twisted the measurement of its cars' carbon emissions, only to then miss out on the electric revolution. At the same time, the OEM became much too dependent on its China activities, developing into a separate minefield on its own, while pivoting aggressively towards Mexico in its North American operations. All these chickens now come home to roost, and Volkswagen owned up to as much when it presented its most recent quarterly report this week. Profits are severely down, market share outside of Germany is stagnating at best, and guidance aims for meagre growth, even before taking into account the looming tariff wars initiated by the Trump administration. Downright irritating, however, is the company's statement of today (March 13th), only two days after its quarterly numbers, that it is going to push its ambitious margin improvement targets three years down the road. Investors are scratching their heads as to why VW didn't say so when they had the chance two days before, thus setting a huge question mark behind its anyway muted guidance. The announcement of new models in the value segments of the market (EV first and foremost) gives at least some comfort, but that will take considerable time to have an effect on VW's prospects. Thus, for the time being, the Wolfsburgers have a decidedly negative balance of pros and cons.
